fixes.realistic_solution: Adam stores two additional states per parameter: the first moment (mean) and second moment (variance), each in FP32 (4 bytes). Combined with the FP32 master weights and FP32 gradients, training requires far more than the weight memory alone. 7B params: 14 GB weights (BF16) + 28 GB master weights (FP32) + 14 GB gradients (BF16) + 56 GB Adam states (2 × 7B × 4 bytes FP32) = ~112 GB — exceeds 80 GB before a single activation is stored.

fixes.napkin_math: Separate kernels per token (hidden_dim=8192, FP16): RMSNorm: read 16 KB + write 16 KB = 32 KB. RoPE: read 16 KB + write 16 KB = 32 KB. QK^T prep: read 16 KB + write 16 KB = 32 KB. Total: 96 KB per token to HBM. Fused kernel: read 16 KB + write 16 KB (QK scores much smaller) ≈ 34 KB per token. Reduction: 96/34 = 2.8x. For batch=256, seq=4096: separate = 256 × 4096 × 96 KB = 100.7 GB HBM traffic. Fused = 256 × 4096 × 34 KB = 35.7 GB. Savings: 65 GB at 3.35 TB/s = 19.4 ms saved per forward pass. Note: per-token HBM traffic is computed over the full hidden_dim (all heads combined), so there is no additional factor for the number of heads.

fixes.realistic_solution: The Ring AllReduce alpha-beta model is: $T = 2(N-1) \cdot \alpha + 2 \cdot \frac{N-1}{N} \cdot M \cdot \beta$, where $N=32$, $M=200\text{ MB}$, $\alpha = 5\,\mu s$, $\beta = 1/50\text{ GB/s} = 0.02\text{ ns/byte}$. Latency component: $2 \times 31 \times 5\,\mu s = 310\,\mu s$. Bandwidth component: $2 \times \frac{31}{32} \times 200\text{ MB} \times 0.02\text{ ns/byte} = 2 \times 0.969 \times 4\text{ ms} = 7.75\text{ ms}$. Total: $\approx 8.06\text{ ms}$. The latency term is ~4% of total — already small. The crossover point (where $\alpha$ term = $\beta$ term) is at message size $M^* = \frac{N \cdot \alpha}{\beta} = 32 \times 5\,\mu s \times 50\text{ GB/s} = 8\text{ MB}$. Below 8 MB, latency dominates and you should use a different algorithm (e.g., recursive halving-doubling).
fixes.napkin_math: - Latency term: 62 steps × 5 μs = 310 μs | - Bandwidth term: 2 × (31/32) × 200 MB at 50 GB/s → 387.5 MB / 50 GB/s = 7.75 ms | - Total ≈ 8.06 ms | - Critical message size: ~8 MB (below this, latency dominates) | - At 200 MB, bandwidth is 96% of total time → Ring is the right choice

- Ring on 64 GPUs: 126 latency steps × 5 μs = 630 μs fixed overhead | - Tree on 64 GPUs: 12 latency steps × 5 μs = 60 μs fixed overhead | - At 256 KB with 50 GB/s links: Ring bandwidth = 2 × (63/64) × 256 KB / 50 GB/s ≈ 10 μs; Tree bandwidth = 2 × log₂(64) × 256 KB / 50 GB/s ≈ 60 μs | - Ring total: 640 μs; Tree total: 120 μs → Tree wins by 5× | - At 100 MB: Ring BW = 2 × (63/64) × 100 MB / 50 GB/s = 3.94 ms; Tree BW = 12 × 100 MB / 50 GB/s = 24 ms → Ring wins by ~5×

Total gradients: 500 layers × 50 KB = 25 MB. Assume 32 GPUs, Ring AllReduce, 50 GB/s InfiniBand. Latency per bucket: 2 × 31 × 5 μs = 310 μs. | 1 MB bucket: 25 buckets. BW per bucket: 2 × (31/32) × 1 MB / 50 GB/s = 39 μs. Per bucket total: 310 + 39 = 349 μs. Serialized: 25 × 349 μs = 8.7 ms. Latency overhead is 89% of total — terrible bandwidth efficiency. | 25 MB bucket (default): 1 bucket. BW: 2 × (31/32) × 25 MB / 50 GB/s = 0.97 ms. Total: 310 μs + 0.97 ms = 1.28 ms. But all 500 layers must finish before the single AllReduce starts — limited overlap with backward. | 100 MB bucket: also 1 bucket (total grads only 25 MB). Same as 25 MB case. | Sweet spot: ~5 MB buckets = 5 buckets. BW per bucket: 194 μs. Per bucket: 504 μs. Buckets fill during backward pass enabling overlap with compute.

GPT-3 175B: ~314 PFLOPS per forward+backward (approx 6 × N × tokens per step). At 1,024 A100s (312 TFLOPS FP16 each), peak = 319 PFLOPS. If MFU = 47%, then step time = 314 PFLOPS / (0.47 × 319 PFLOPS) ≈ 2.1s. With activation checkpointing adding 33% recompute: actual hardware FLOPS = 314 × 1.33 = 418 PFLOPS per step. HFU = 418 / (319 × 2.1) = 418 / 670 ≈ 62%. The 15pp gap (62% − 47%) means roughly (62−47)/62 = 24% of the hardware's work is 'wasted' on recomputation. MFU is the right metric for comparing training configurations because it normalizes out implementation choices like checkpointing depth.

Naive 50/50 split: Team A can run min(50/1, 500/50) = 10 jobs, using 10 GPUs and 500 CPUs. 40 GPUs sit idle. Team B can run min(50/4, 500/10) = 12 jobs, using 48 GPUs and 120 CPUs. 380 CPUs sit idle. Total utilization: 58 GPUs (58%), 620 CPUs (62%). DRF allocation: Team A's dominant resource is CPU (5% per job). Team B's dominant resource is GPU (4% per job). Equalize dominant shares: let A run x jobs, B run y jobs. x/20 = y/25 → y = 5x/4. Constraints: x + 4y ≤ 100 and 50x + 10y ≤ 1000. Substituting: 6x ≤ 100 and 62.5x ≤ 1000 → x = 16, y = 20. Team A: 16 jobs (16 GPUs, 800 CPUs). Team B: 20 jobs (80 GPUs, 200 CPUs). Total: 96 GPUs (96%), 1000 CPUs (100%). DRF nearly saturates the cluster vs. the naive split's ~60% utilization.

fixes.realistic_solution: Three factors explain the gap. First, while the parameter reduction is 8× (16.8M → 2.1M), the FLOP reduction is also 8× for matrix-vector products. The original matrix-vector multiply costs $2 \times 4096^2 = 33.6$ MFLOPs. The two low-rank multiplies cost $2 \times 4096 \times 256 + 2 \times 256 \times 4096 = 4.2$ MFLOPs — an 8× reduction. However, 8× fewer FLOPs does not yield 8× speedup. Second, the two sequential matrix multiplies have lower arithmetic intensity than one large multiply. The original $4096 \times 4096$ matmul has high arithmetic intensity and saturates Tensor Cores; each low-rank multiply ($4096 \times 256$) is a tall-skinny or skinny-tall matrix that may not fill the Tensor Core tile efficiently, reducing hardware utilization from ~70% to ~25%. Third, you now have two kernel launches instead of one, adding overhead and preventing the GPU from amortizing the memory-bandwidth cost of reading activations — the activation vector is read from HBM twice instead of once.
fixes.napkin_math: Original: $4096 \times 4096$ matmul = 33.6M FLOPs, one kernel launch, activations read once. Decomposed: $4096 \times 256$ + $256 \times 4096$ = 2.1M + 2.1M = 4.2M FLOPs, two kernel launches. FLOP ratio: 8× fewer FLOPs. Arithmetic intensity of $4096 \times 256$ matmul: much lower than $4096 \times 4096$. On Orin with 275 TOPS INT8 but only ~25% utilization on skinny matmuls → effective throughput drops from ~110 TOPS to ~27 TOPS. Net real speedup: roughly $8 \times (27/110) \approx 2\times$, explaining the ~15-20% latency improvement (the matmul is only part of the layer's total compute).

fixes.realistic_solution: Using a two-proportion z-test, detecting a 5pp difference (94% vs 89%) with 80% power and α=0.05 requires approximately 484 samples per subgroup. For 50 intersectional subgroups: 50 × 484 = 24,200 minimum test samples, assuming perfect balance. Real-world subgroup distributions are highly skewed (intersectional minorities may be <1% of data), so you need to oversample rare subgroups. Practical minimum: ~60K–100K labeled evaluation images with stratified sampling. The systems implication: disaggregated evaluation is a data engineering problem. You need a metadata-rich evaluation dataset with demographic annotations, a stratified sampling pipeline, and statistical testing infrastructure that reports per-subgroup confidence intervals, not just point estimates.
fixes.napkin_math: Sample size for two-proportion test: $n \approx \frac{(z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})^2 \times (p_1(1-p_1) + p_2(1-p_2))}{(p_1 - p_2)^2}$. With $p_1=0.94$, $p_2=0.89$, $z_{0.025}=1.96$, $z_{0.2}=0.84$: $n \approx \frac{(1.96+0.84)^2 \times (0.0564 + 0.0979)}{0.0025} = \frac{7.84 \times 0.1543}{0.0025} \approx 484$ per group. 50 subgroups × 484 = 24,200 samples minimum.

Assume D2D bandwidth is 50 GB/s per link (realistic for silicon interposer). Workload B requires moving a 16K × 16K partial sum matrix in FP16 = 512 MB per reduction step. For a 16K×16K×16K GEMM (≈8.5 TFLOPs), compute time across 4 chiplets at 800 TFLOPS peak = 8.5e12 / 800e12 = 10.6 ms. However, transferring 512 MB over 50 GB/s D2D takes 10.2 ms per step; with 3 reduction steps across 4 chiplets, total D2D time ≈ 30.7 ms. The operation is heavily D2D-bound: total time ≈ 41 ms, yielding 8.5e12 / 41e-3 ≈ 207 TFLOPS effective (vs 800 TFLOPS peak). By contrast, Workload A (element-wise) requires zero D2D traffic and runs at full throughput.

200B model in BF16 = 400 GB. With ZeRO-3, each GPU stores 1/8 of the parameters on CPU RAM = 50 GB. During forward + backward, each GPU must fetch its 50 GB shard from CPU over PCIe and participate in all-gathers. PCIe Gen5 x16 effective bandwidth ≈ 50 GB/s. Fetching 50 GB per pass × 2 passes (fwd + bwd) = 100 GB over PCIe. Transfer time: 100 GB / 50 GB/s = 2.0 seconds. Compute time: 6 × 200e9 × 8192 tokens / (8 × 989 TFLOPS) ≈ 1.25 seconds. Total step time ≈ 2.0 + 1.25 + overhead ≈ 4.5 seconds — consistent with the observed 4500 ms. The system is ~60% PCIe-bound. NVLink sits idle because the bottleneck is CPU→GPU, not GPU↔GPU.

Between-home variance (σ) is $50/month. Model impact (δ) is expected to be $5/month. For a two-sample test with 80% power (z_β=0.84) and α=0.05 (z_α/2=1.96): N per group = 2 × (1.96+0.84)² × 50² / 5² = 2 × 7.84 × 100 = 1,568 homes per group (~3,200 total). Within-home variance (day-to-day) is only $5/month. Switchback testing reduces the required N: 2 × 7.84 × 5² / 5² = 2 × 7.84 ≈ 16 per group — roughly 100× fewer devices needed, or equivalently, dramatically higher statistical power with the same sample size.

128k context, 7B model (32 layers, 32 KV heads, head_dim=128). FP16 KV cache = 2 × 32 × 32 × 128 × 128,000 × 2 bytes = 67.1 GB. INT4 reduces each value from 2 bytes to 0.5 bytes → 67.1 × (0.5/2) = 16.8 GB. Group-size-128 quantization adds 16-bit scale/zero-point metadata per group: 128,000 × (32 × 128 / 128) × 32 × 2 × 2 × 2 bytes ≈ 524 MB of overhead — minimal compared to the 16.8 GB cache, but drastically reduces quantization error for needle retrieval.

Pre-computed: For d=128, context N=128k. HBM read per token = 4 × 128 × 2 bytes (Q, K, Sin, Cos) = 1 KB. Total read = 128k × 1 KB = 128 MB. Time at 2 TB/s = ~64 μs. Recompute: Read only Q, K (512 bytes/token). Total read = 64 MB. Time = ~32 μs. Compute for sin/cos = ~20 FLOPs per element × 128 dims × 128k tokens = 327 MFLOPs. Compute time at 312 TFLOPS = 327e6 / 312e12 ≈ 1.05 μs — completely hidden by the 32 μs memory load time. Net effect: ~2× speedup by eliminating half the HBM reads, with recompute cost invisible under the memory latency.

Consider a layer with compute-bound MatMul (0.32 ms) and memory-bound ops like LayerNorm/GeLU/Dropout (0.50 ms). Forward time = 0.82 ms. Backward time ≈ 1.0 ms (MatMul backward ≈ 0.64 ms + memory-bound ops backward ≈ 0.36 ms — backward of memory ops is cheaper since gradients flow through without re-reading full activations). No checkpoint: total = 0.82 + 1.0 = 1.82 ms per layer. With checkpoint: recompute forward adds 0.82 ms, BUT the recomputed activations must be written to HBM then immediately re-read for backward, adding ~0.25 ms of extra memory traffic that the non-checkpointed path avoids (activations were already resident). New total = 0.82 + 0.82 + 0.25 + 1.0 = 2.89 ms. Overhead: 2.89/1.82 = 1.59× — a 59% increase, far exceeding the theoretical 33% FLOP-based estimate. The extra memory round-trips for recomputed activations across all layers compound into the observed 75% overhead.

For a 3B model, assume 28 layers, 4 KV heads (GQA), head_dim=64, INT8 KV cache on-device. KV per token = 2 (K+V) × 28 layers × 4 heads × 64 dim × 1 byte = 14.3 KB. SRAM capacity = 4 MB. For 500 tokens: KV cache = 500 × 14.3 KB ≈ 7 MB — fits partially in SRAM with tiled execution, keeping active layers' KV resident. For 1500 tokens: KV cache = 1500 × 14.3 KB ≈ 21 MB — far exceeds 4 MB SRAM. The O(N²) attention intermediate for 1500 tokens (1500² × 4 heads × 2 bytes ≈ 72 MB per layer) forces continuous paging between SRAM and LPDDR5 DRAM, dropping effective compute utilization from ~80% to under 15% and explaining the non-linear TTFT spike.

For a 4B model, assume 36 layers, 8 KV heads (GQA), head_dim=128. With a 1000-token context: INT8 KV cache per layer = 2 × 8 × 128 × 1000 × 1 byte = 2 MB. Total across 36 layers = 72 MB (INT8), vs 144 MB (FP16) — saving ~72 MB, though the scenario's 600 MB savings implies a longer context or larger head config. Dequantization overhead per layer: read 2 MB (INT8) + write 4 MB (FP16 intermediate) = 6 MB. At 30 GB/s LPDDR bandwidth: 0.2 ms per layer. Across 36 layers: 7.2 ms. Kernel launch overhead on mobile (~0.2 ms per extra dequant kernel × 36 layers): ~7.2 ms. Total added latency ≈ 14–20 ms per token, explaining the increase from 35 ms to 55 ms.

KV cache per token for Llama-2 13B (40 layers, 40 KV heads, head_dim=128, FP16): 2 (K+V) × 40 × 40 × 128 × 2 bytes = 819,200 bytes ≈ 800 KB per token. This already accounts for all layers. Block size 512 tokens = 512 × 800 KB = 400 MB per block. For batch=128 requests (avg 80 tokens each): reserved = 128 blocks × 400 MB = 50 GB. Actual needed: 128 × 80 × 800 KB = 8 GB. Wasted: 42 GB — causes OOM on an 80 GB GPU (model weights ~26 GB + 50 GB reserved = 76 GB, no room for activations). Block size 16: each request needs ceil(80/16) = 5 blocks × 16 × 800 KB = 64 MB. Total reserved: 128 × 64 MB = 8.2 GB ≈ actual usage. Problem solved without extra hardware.

1000 QPS = 86.4M req/day. Cloud-only cost = $86,400/day. To meet P95 < 100ms, at most 5% of requests can be routed to the cloud (since cloud latency = 270ms > 100ms). At 5% cloud routing: cloud traffic = 50 QPS, cost = $4,320/day (saving $82,080/day). Average latency = 0.95 × 20ms + 0.05 × 270ms = 19 + 13.5 = 32.5ms. P95 = 20ms (since only 5% go to cloud, the 95th percentile still hits the local path). For accuracy: the edge model at 85% overall can achieve ~96% accuracy on its top-95% most confident predictions (confidence-based routing selects the hardest 5% for the cloud). Overall accuracy = 0.95 × 96% + 0.05 × 99% = 91.2% + 5.0% = 96.1%, meeting the >95% target.
fixes.realistic_solution: Implement a dynamic cascading confidence threshold. The edge device runs the lightweight model first and extracts a confidence score (e.g., max softmax probability or entropy). If the confidence exceeds a tuned threshold, the device returns the local prediction immediately. If it falls below the threshold, the query is routed to the cloud. The critical constraint: to meet the P95 < 100ms SLA, the cloud routing rate must stay at or below 5%, since any cloud-routed request exceeds 100ms. This means the confidence threshold must be set to reject only the bottom ~5% of predictions. The co-design challenge is calibrating the edge model's uncertainty estimates so that the 5% routed to the cloud captures the most error-prone queries, maximizing the accuracy uplift per cloud dollar spent.

fixes.napkin_math: Base sleep current (CPU + peripherals) = 1.0 μA. Each 64 KB SRAM bank costs 3.3 μA for data retention. The 110 KB model weights live in flash (read-only, no retention cost). Runtime working memory (inference buffers, feature maps) occupies ~50 KB in SRAM0. Original power: 1.0 μA base + 1 bank (SRAM0) × 3.3 μA = 4.3 μA. After adding FL: the 12 KB gradient buffer is placed by the linker into SRAM2 (SRAM1 is unused but the linker's default heap grows into SRAM2). Now SRAM0 + SRAM2 must stay powered. But the power controller works in contiguous ranges — to retain SRAM2, it must also power SRAM1. Retention: 1.0 + 3 banks × 3.3 = 10.9 μA. Fix: force the linker to place the FL buffer into SRAM0's remaining 14 KB → only 1 bank retained → back to 4.3 μA.

Active radio power = 2.5 W. RRC tail: radio stays at 1.2 W for ~4 seconds after each transmission = 4.8 J per tail event. Original schedule: 1 transfer of 50 MB every 100 steps. Active time at 10 MB/s = 5 s → 12.5 J active + 4.8 J tail = 17.3 J per 100 steps. New schedule: 10 transfers of 5 MB every 10 steps. Each transfer: 0.5 s active → 1.25 J + 4.8 J tail = 6.05 J. Total for 10 transfers = 60.5 J per 100 steps. Energy increase: 60.5 / 17.3 = 3.5× (250% increase). The tail energy (48 J total) now dominates, accounting for 79% of the new energy budget despite total bytes transferred remaining constant. The lesson: on cellular networks, transmission frequency matters more than payload size.

Compute node (8 × 650 W + 2 kW host) = 7.2 kW. Inference node (8 × 350 W + 2 kW host) = 4.8 kW. Homogeneous compute: 2 nodes = 14.4 kW, 16 GPUs. Homogeneous inference: 3 nodes = 14.4 kW, 24 GPUs. Mixed rack: 1 compute node (7.2 kW) + 1 inference node (4.8 kW) = 12.0 kW, 16 GPUs with 3 kW headroom. This serves both workload types from one rack. Better: dynamic power-aware scheduling. When compute jobs finish, those GPUs shift to inference workload (350 W), dropping the node from 7.2 kW to 4.8 kW. This frees power budget for bursting other nodes. Peak: 2 × 7.2 = 14.4 kW (16 compute GPUs). Off-peak: 2 × 4.8 = 9.6 kW (16 inference GPUs) with 5.4 kW headroom for a 3rd partial node. Time-multiplexing power budgets across workload phases is more effective than static mixed allocation.
